title: Ναυαγὸς καὶ θάλασσα
titleEn: The Shipwrecked Man and the Sea
source: Émile Chambry, Ésope: Fables, texte établi et traduit (Les Belles Lettres, Paris, 1927), fable 245 (= Perry 168). Text as printed, including Chambry's editorial punctuation (« », ⟨ ⟩ for editorial supplements). Pronunciation: reconstructed Attic, after W. S. Allen, Vox Graeca. Romanization: standard classical transliteration (ē/ō; th/ph/ch; y for υ, u in diphthongs; h = rough breathing; ēi/āi/ōi for iota subscript; accents omitted).

sentences.t: Ναυαγὸς ἐκβρασθεὶς εἰς τὸν αἰγιαλὸν ἐκοιμᾶτο διὰ τὸν κόπον·
sentences.tr: Nauagos ekbrastheis eis ton aigialon ekoimato dia ton kopon:
sentences.l: A shipwrecked-man, cast-up onto the shore, was-sleeping because-of the exhaustion:
sentences.i: A shipwrecked man, washed up on the beach, lay asleep, worn out.
sentences.n: ναυαγός — 'ship-breaker(ed)': ναῦς + ἄγνυμι 'break'; the wreck is in the word. ἐκβρασθείς, 'boiled out, cast up' — the sea spits him ashore like surf.

sentences.t: μετὰ μικρὸν δὲ ἐξαναστάς, ὡς ἐθεάσατο τὴν θάλασσαν, ἐμέμφετο αὐτῇ ὅτι γε δελεάζουσα τοὺς ἀνθρώπους τῇ πραότητι τῆς συνόψεως, ἡνίκα ἂν αὐτοὺς προσδέξηται, ἀπαγριουμένη διαφθείρει.
sentences.tr: meta mikron de exanastas, hōs etheasato tēn thalassan, emempheto autēi hoti ge deleazousa tous anthrōpous tēi praotēti tēs synopseōs, hēnika an autous prosdexētai, apagrioumenē diaphtheirei.
sentences.l: and after a-little having-risen-up, when he-beheld the sea, he-reproached her that, luring men by-the gentleness of-her look, whenever she-receives them, turning-savage she-destroys-them.
sentences.i: Waking a little later and seeing the sea, he began reproaching her: she lures men with the gentleness of her looks, and then, once she has taken them in, turns savage and destroys them.
sentences.n: δελεάζουσα — from δέλεαρ, 'bait': the sea as angler. ἡνίκα ἄν + subjunctive: 'whenever' — the charge is habitual, not a single storm.

sentences.t: Ἡ δὲ ὁμοιωθεῖσα γυναικὶ ἔφη πρὸς αὐτόν· « Ἀλλ', ὦ οὗτος, μὴ ἐμὲ μέμφου, ἀλλὰ τοὺς ἀνέμους· ἐγὼ μὲν γὰρ φύσει τοιαύτη εἰμὶ ὁποίαν καὶ νῦν με ὁρᾷς· οἱ δὲ αἰφνίδιόν με ἐπέρχονται καὶ κυματοῦσι καὶ ἐξαγριοῦσιν. »
sentences.tr: Hē de homoiōtheisa gynaiki ephē pros auton: « All', ō houtos, mē eme memphou, alla tous anemous: egō men gar physei toiautē eimi hopoian kai nyn me horāis: hoi de aiphnidion me eperchontai kai kymatousi kai exagriousin. »
sentences.l: And she, having-been-likened to-a-woman, said to him: « But, you there, not me blame, but the winds: for I by-nature such am as even now me you-see: but they suddenly me come-upon and raise-to-waves and make-savage. »
sentences.i: The sea, taking a woman's form, said to him: « Do not blame me, friend — blame the winds. By nature I am just as you see me now; it is they who fall on me without warning, whip me to waves, and turn me wild. »
sentences.n: ὁμοιωθεῖσα γυναικί — the sea appears as a woman to plead her case: a rare mythic touch in these prose fables. Her defense is the fable's φύσις-argument turned upside down: my nature is the calm; the violence is imported.

sentences.t: Ἀτὰρ οὖν καὶ ἡμᾶς ἐπὶ τῶν ἀδικημάτων οὐ δεῖ τοὺς δρῶντας αἰτιᾶσθαι, ὅταν ἑτέροις ὑποτεταγμένοι ὦσι, τοὺς δὲ τούτοις ἐπιστατοῦντας.
sentences.tr: Atar oun kai hēmas epi tōn adikēmatōn ou dei tous drōntas aitiāsthai, hotan heterois hypotetagmenoi ōsi, tous de toutois epistatountas.
sentences.l: So then also us, in-the-case of-wrongs, it-must not the doers blame, whenever to-others subordinated they-be, but those over-them standing-in-command.
sentences.i: So we too, when wrong is done, should blame not the doers — when they act under others' orders — but the ones who command them.
sentences.n: A moral in the first person plural (καὶ ἡμᾶς … οὐ δεῖ): the fable turns and includes us. τοὺς δρῶντας … τοὺς ἐπιστατοῦντας: agents versus principals — the sea's defense generalized into a rule about responsibility.
